article_title: FortiBleed Campaign Exposes Credentials for Roughly 86,000 FortiGate Devices Across 194 Countries as CISA Urges Immediate Password Resets
summary: Clean, well-attributed report on the FortiBleed FortiGate credential-harvesting campaign; conflicting vendor figures each correctly attributed inline to the single outlet carrying them; Diachenko quote verbatim; no claim rests on a bot-blocked source.

content_preview.title: FortiBleed Campaign Exposes Credentials for Roughly 86,000 FortiGate Devices Across 194 Countries as CISA Urges Immediate Password Resets
content_preview.summary: A credential-harvesting campaign dubbed FortiBleed reached 86,644 compromised FortiGate devices across 194 countries by June 19, prompting a CISA hardening advisory.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A large-scale credential-harvesting campaign against internet-facing Fortinet firewalls, dubbed FortiBleed, had climbed to 86,644 unique compromised devices by June 19, 2026, according to [Shattered Security](https://shattered.io/fortibleed-fortinet-86644-firewalls-2026/). The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) issued a hardening advisory on June 18 urging Fortinet customers to terminate active sessions and reset credentials, as reported by [SecurityWeek](h...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains (arcticwolf.com, recordedfuture.com) to config/source_allowlist.txt for future reviews.

Cyber Security News (cybersecuritynews.com) bot-blocks the snapshotter (HTTP 403); the same facts it is cited for are independently corroborated in the Shattered and SecurityWeek snapshots, so no claim rests on the unverifiable source.

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (683 words). Overview / What We Know / Scale / What We Don't Know / Analysis sections. Technical depth is accurate (config-file harvesting, offline SHA-256 cracking, PBKDF2 migration in FortiOS 7.2.11/7.4.8/7.6.1, 45-GPU Hashtopolis cluster). The 'What We Don't Know' section honestly explains why vendor figures diverge by counting methodology.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all five available gzipped snapshots from disk; the sixth (Cyber Security News) returned HTTP 403 and could not be snapshotted. source-0 (Arctic Wolf): confirms SOCRadar 30,000-75,000 working creds across 194 countries, PBKDF2 introduction in FortiOS 7.2.11/7.4.8/7.6.1, legacy SHA-256 storage, 'log in after upgrading' and MFA recommendations. source-1 (SecurityWeek): confirms the Diachenko quote VERBATIM ('They intercept SSL VPN authentication, crack hashes on a 45-GPU cluster managed via Hashtopolis, and pivot into internal Active Directory environments'), Beaumont + Hudson Rock independent verification, CISA alert issued Thursday (June 18) with the exact hardening actions listed, and 'over 86,000' / 86,644 figures. source-2 (CSO Online): confirms ~75,000 firewalls, ~50% of Shodan-visible Fortinet firewalls, 194 countries, 21,000+ domains, India/US/Mexico top countries, the 'systematically collected configuration files... recover working administrator credentials' quote verbatim, and the SHA-256-until-login storage detail. source-3 (Recorded Future): confirms 73,932 FortiGate firewall URLs across 194 countries, ~1.16 billion credential attempts against 320,777 targets, 45-GPU Hashtopolis cluster, Insikt Group, Russian-speaking attribution, Diachenko June 13 discovery, and the rotate-credentials/MFA recommendations. source-5 (Shattered Security): confirms 86,644 unique compromised devices by June 19, 194 countries, 'one of the most globally distributed credential-exposure events', Diachenko June 13 discovery, and the FG-IR-26-060 / FG-IR-25-647 recycled-credential codes. source-4 (Cyber Security News) HTTP 403 — UNVERIFIABLE via snapshot; the three facts it is cited for (CISA advisory, FG-IR codes, 'up to 86,000') are each independently confirmed in verified snapshots (SecurityWeek and Shattered), so no claim depends on it.
editor_notes.attribution_discipline: Verified the core concern: each conflicting vendor figure is attributed inline to the single outlet that carries it, with NO compound citations stacking one number onto multiple outlets. SOCRadar 30,000-75,000 -> Arctic Wolf (correct). ~75,000 / 50% Shodan / India-US-Mexico -> CSO Online (correct). 73,932 URLs / 1.16B attempts / 320,777 targets -> Recorded Future (correct). 'up to 86,000' -> Cyber Security News (corroborated by SecurityWeek). 86,644 unique by June 19 / 194 countries / most globally distributed -> Shattered Security (correct). Each number maps cleanly to its source.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every number, name, code, quote, and date traces to a cited source and matches the snapshot. No hallucinations detected. CISA's primary alert and The Hacker News are NOT cited inline; no headline/summary/lead claim rests on a bot-blocked source.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference. The 'one of the most globally distributed credential-exposure events on record' phrasing is sourced to Shattered Security and Recorded Future, not editorial embellishment.
editor_notes.originality: Distinct from the two prior Fortinet articles. The Feb 23, 2026 piece covered a different actor using LLMs to breach 600 FortiGate devices across 55 countries (Amazon Threat Intelligence). The June 01, 2026 piece covered CVE-2026-35616 in FortiClient EMS (a different product) delivering EKZ infostealer. FortiBleed is a new, distinct credential-harvesting campaign at far larger scale with different attribution and methodology. No duplication.
recommendations: Add arcticwolf.com and recordedfuture.com to the source allowlist to reduce false-positive warnings in future reviews.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, accurate, well-attributed submission. The automated script suggested APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS, but its two warnings are infrastructure flags (allowlist gap + one bot-blocked snapshot), not content defects: every claim the 403 source is cited for is independently corroborated in a snapshot I read, and the conflicting vendor figures are each correctly attributed inline. No correction is honestly needed. Verdict: APPROVE.
